headline: Sabah paused a federal rollout. Federal courts paused Sabah's 40% payout.
context: On 13 May 2026, Sabah deferred the federal AKPS border agency citing MA63 immigration autonomy. On 6 April, the Court of Appeal stayed payment on Sabah's 40% revenue: a case the High Court ruled went 48 years without review.

cards.big: Sabah just paused a federal rollout. Federal courts paused Sabah's 40% payout 5 weeks earlier.
cards.sub: On 13 May Sabah deferred AKPS. On 6 April the Court of Appeal stayed payment on a 40% grant the High Court ruled had gone 48 years without review.

cards.big: On 6 April 2026 the Court of Appeal granted Putrajaya's stay on Sabah's 40% revenue payment pending appeal.
cards.sub: AGC said the appeal contests the High Court's finding of breach by BOTH governments since 1974, not the 40% formula itself.

cards.big: On 13 May 2026 Sabah deferred AKPS, citing clashes between Act 860 and the Immigration Act 1959/63.
cards.sub: CM Hajiji called Sabah's MA63 immigration autonomy 'non-negotiable' and flagged Section 6(3) as giving the state only indirect AKPS oversight.

cards.big: The 40% grant is constitutional, but excludes Sabah's largest revenue: oil and gas, ring-fenced under the Petroleum Development Act 1974.
cards.sub: Articles 112C/112D cover 'net revenue derived from Sabah.' Oil and gas sit under Petronas and a 5% royalty, not the 40% grant.

cards.big: Within 48 hours, Sabah's Law Society, immigration officers' union, and ex-CM Salleh Said Keruak backed the AKPS deferral.
cards.sub: SLS called it 'legal foresight'. Salleh called it 'prudent and justified'. Both said MA63 immigration autonomy is constitutional, not administrative.

cards.big: The High Court found both governments in breach since 1974. Putrajaya pauses the remedy; Sabah pauses a new law before it operates.
Two pauses, two clocks. Putrajaya extends a 48-year delay the court called unconstitutional. Sabah stops a new law before it operates.
